name: Tomahawk Cruise Missile
description: Ship and submarine-launched cruise missile. The workhorse of the opening salvo — 800+ fired in the first weeks, each one costing more than most Americans earn in a lifetime. GPS/INS guided with 1,000-mile range.
imageDesc: Tomahawk missile launching from a Navy destroyer at night, trailing fire across dark water

name: JASSM-ER (AGM-158B)
description: Air-launched stealth cruise missile designed to evade advanced air defenses. Fired from B-1B and F-15E aircraft against hardened Iranian targets including IRGC command bunkers and air defense sites.
imageDesc: Stealth cruise missile dropping from a bomber bay, sleek dark body against clouds

name: JDAM Kit (GBU-31/32/38)
description: GPS guidance kit bolted onto dumb bombs to make them 'smart.' At $25K each they're the cheapest precision weapon — so they get used the most. 5,000+ dropped on Iranian military infrastructure, vehicles, and troop concentrations.
imageDesc: Row of olive-green GPS-guided bombs loaded under a fighter jet wing

name: B-2 Spirit Bomber
description: Stealth strategic bomber at $2.1 billion each — the most expensive aircraft ever built. Used to penetrate Iranian air defenses and deliver bunker-busting bombs on underground nuclear facilities. At $130K per flight hour, every sortie costs more than a house.
imageDesc: B-2 stealth bomber flying wing silhouette against sunset sky

name: B-1B Lancer
description: Supersonic heavy bomber carrying the largest conventional payload in the US arsenal. Used extensively as a JASSM-ER launch platform, loitering outside Iranian airspace and firing cruise missiles. $60K per flight hour.
imageDesc: B-1B bomber with wings swept back during low-altitude supersonic run

name: F-35A Lightning II
description: 5th-gen stealth multirole fighter. The Pentagon's most expensive weapons program ($1.7T lifetime cost). Deployed for SEAD missions and precision strikes in Iran. At $36K/flight hour, it costs $36,000 every time the wheels leave the ground — per hour.
imageDesc: F-35A in flight with weapons bay doors open revealing internal missiles

name: F-22 Raptor
description: Air superiority fighter ensuring US dominance of Iranian skies. At $150M each, only 187 were ever built. Now flying combat air patrols over Iran — the first peer-level air war it was designed for.
imageDesc: F-22 Raptor performing a high-G turn with vapor trails streaming from wing edges

name: F/A-18 Super Hornet
description: Carrier-based strike fighter — the backbone of Navy air operations against Iran. Launching from carriers in the Persian Gulf and Arabian Sea, Super Hornets fly strike and escort missions around the clock.
imageDesc: F/A-18 launching from aircraft carrier catapult with afterburners lit

name: MQ-9 Reaper Drone
description: Armed surveillance drone — the signature weapon of the 'War on Terror' now deployed over Iran. Can loiter for 27 hours carrying Hellfire missiles and JDAMs. Each drone costs $32M; the missiles it fires cost extra.
imageDesc: MQ-9 Reaper drone in flight over arid terrain with Hellfire missiles under wings

name: RQ-4 Global Hawk
description: High-altitude surveillance drone flying at 60,000 feet for 30+ hours. Iran shot one down in 2019 — now they're back, scanning every square mile of Iranian territory. At $131M each, losing one costs more than an F-35.
imageDesc: RQ-4 Global Hawk unmanned aircraft at extreme altitude above cloud layer

name: Patriot PAC-3 Missile
description: Hit-to-kill interceptor shooting down Iranian ballistic missiles aimed at US bases and allied capitals. At $4M per shot against missiles that cost Iran $300K, the math is brutal — and intentional. Iran fires cheap missiles to drain expensive interceptors.
imageDesc: Patriot missile battery launching interceptor at night with bright exhaust trail

name: THAAD Interceptor
description: Terminal High Altitude missile defense interceptor. Deployed in Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Qatar to shield against Iranian medium-range ballistic missiles. At $12M per interceptor, each successful shoot-down costs more than most hospitals.
imageDesc: THAAD launcher firing interceptor vertically from desert deployment site

name: SM-3 (Standard Missile-3)
description: Aegis ship-based ballistic missile interceptor. Fired from destroyers and cruisers in the Persian Gulf to intercept Iranian missiles in their midcourse phase. At $15M each, the Navy is spending more on interceptors than Iran spends on the missiles being intercepted.
imageDesc: SM-3 missile launching from Aegis destroyer with dramatic sea spray

name: GBU-57 MOP (Massive Ordnance Penetrator)
description: 30,000-pound bunker buster — the largest non-nuclear bomb in the US arsenal. Designed specifically for Iran's underground nuclear facilities at Fordow and Natanz. Penetrates 200+ feet of reinforced concrete. Only the B-2 can carry it.
imageDesc: Massive 30,000-lb bomb being loaded into B-2 bomber cargo bay by ground crew

name: AGM-88 HARM
description: High-speed Anti-Radiation Missile that homes in on radar emissions. Used to destroy Iranian air defense radars — blinding their SAM network so bombers can operate freely. The opening move in any air campaign.
imageDesc: AGM-88 HARM missile under fighter jet wing with anti-radiation seeker visible

name: Mk 48 Torpedo
description: Heavyweight submarine-launched torpedo used against Iranian navy vessels and mini-submarines in the Strait of Hormuz. At $3.8M each, these are the most expensive torpedoes ever made.
imageDesc: Mk 48 torpedo being loaded into submarine torpedo tube by sailors

name: Harpoon Anti-Ship Missile
description: Sea-skimming anti-ship missile used to sink Iranian fast attack boats and frigates in the Persian Gulf. A 40-year-old design still punching holes in ships.
imageDesc: Harpoon missile skimming over ocean surface toward target ship

name: HIMARS (M142)
description: High Mobility Artillery Rocket System — made famous in Ukraine, now deployed to forward bases in Iraq and Gulf states for precision rocket strikes into western Iran. Each launcher fires 6 GPS-guided rockets or 1 ATACMS tactical missile.
imageDesc: HIMARS launcher firing rocket salvo from desert position at dusk

name: Stinger Missile (FIM-92)
description: Man-portable air defense missile (MANPADS) deployed for base defense against Iranian drones and low-flying aircraft. The same weapon that helped the Afghan mujahideen shoot down Soviet helicopters in the 1980s.
imageDesc: Soldier shouldering Stinger missile launcher aimed at sky from fortified position

name: Javelin Anti-Tank Missile
description: Fire-and-forget anti-tank missile with top-attack capability. Not a primary weapon in this conflict but deployed with ground forces in Iraq and at forward bases as defense against Iranian armor and vehicles.
imageDesc: Javelin missile system with CLU targeting unit on tripod in desert setting

name: AGM-114 Hellfire
description: Precision air-to-ground missile fired from drones, helicopters, and aircraft. The go-to weapon for targeted strikes — each one designed to kill a vehicle, a building, or a person for $150K.
imageDesc: Hellfire missile mounted under Apache helicopter stub wing

name: SDB (GBU-39 Small Diameter Bomb)
description: 250-lb GPS-guided bomb with standoff glide wings. Designed to minimize collateral damage in urban areas — though 'minimize' is doing heavy lifting. F-35s carry 8 internally.
imageDesc: Small diameter bombs loaded in internal weapons bay of stealth fighter
